Name: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers ('HTTP Request/Response Splitting')
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product receives data from an HTTP agent/component (e.g., web server, proxy, browser, etc.), but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes CR and LF characters before the data is included in outgoing HTTP headers.
Scenario: An attacker can make a single request to a vulnerable server that will cause the server to create two responses, the second of which may be misinterpreted as a response to a different request, possibly one made by another user sharing the same TCP connection with the server.
Security Impact: modify application data, gain privileges, cache poisoning, page hijacking, http response splitting
Alexander root cause analysis: Lack of knowledge / Rule encoding deficiencies

Name: Externally-Generated Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product performs an operation that triggers an external diagnostic or error message that is not directly generated or controlled by the product, such as an error generated by the programming language interpreter that a software application uses. The error can contain sensitive system information.
Scenario: if the code does not catch runtime exceptions, meaning that if such an exception were to occur, the error messge container may display potentially dangerous information (such as a full stack trace).
Security Impact: read application data, sensitive information, system information
Security Related Design: system configuration, compilation hardening, error handling, exceptions

Name: uncontrolled recursion
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product does not properly control the amount of recursion that takes place, consuming excessive resources, such as allocated memory or the program stack.
Scenario: a mistake exists in the code where the exit condition contained in flg is never called. This results in the function calling itself over and over again until the stack is exhausted.
Security Impact: consuming memory, stack exhaustion, read application data, system crash
Alexander root cause analysis: Rule encoding deficiencies / lack of knowledge

Name: Missing Standard Error Handling Mechanism
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product does not use a standardized method for handling errors throughout the code, which might introduce inconsistent error handling and resultant weaknesses.
Scenario: An error during execution occurs but a lack of error handling is found for this code block.
Security Related Design: standardized method, error handling, exceptional conditions

Name: Bad Practices: Use of System.exit()
Author: Jasmine
Description: A J2EE application uses System.exit(), which also shuts down its container.
Scenario: a program using system.exit() in the event of a specific exception error for web application.
Security Impact: system crash, system exit, system restart, denial of service attack

Name: Double Free
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product calls free() twice on the same memory address.
Scenario: When programmer frees a memory space when they already had done so prior.
Security Impact: modify memory, execute unauthorized code, corrupted memory, system crash, buffer overflow attack
Security Related Design: automatic memory management, automated static analysis, code quality, fuzz testing
Root cause 1: CM1: forgetting something has been done
Alexander root cause analysis: CM1: Forgetting something has been done

Name: Misinterpretation of Input
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product misinterprets an input, whether from an attacker or another product, in a security-relevant fashion.
Scenario: The program is led to crash or in an incorrect result from the misinterpretation of data.
Security Impact: unexpected state, incorrect interpretation, behavioral problems

Name: Ommitted break statement in switch
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product omits a break statement within a switch or similar construct, causing code associated with multiple conditions to execute. This can cause problems when the programmer only intended to execute code associated with one condition.
Scenario: When a programmer writes a switch statement in code but neglects a break statement within the case.
Security Impact: unexpected application behavior, critical code executing, behavioral problems

Name: Loop with unreachable exit condition
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product contains an iteration or loop with an exit condition that cannot be reached
Scenario: Program runs forever with an eventual program crash from an infinite loop within code. Possibly from a for, while loop, or recursion loop.
Security Impact: infinite loop, resource consumption, excessive iteration, behavioral problems

Name: Comparison using wrong factors
Author: Jasmine
Description: The code performs a comparison between two entities, but the comparison examines the wrong factors or characteristics of the entities, which can lead to incorrect results and resultant weaknesses.
Scenario: the code might inadvertently compare references to objects, instead of the relevant contents of those objects, causing two "equal" objects to be considered unequal.
Security Impact: incorrect results, result weakness, behavioral problems, comparison errors

Name: Unverified Ownership
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product does not properly verify that a critical resource is owned by the proper entity.
Scenario: A product needs to verify ownership of a user before giving them admin privilages.
Security Related Design: properly verify ownership, separation of privileges

Name: Use of a key past its expiration date.
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product uses a cryptographic key or password past its expiration date, which diminishes its safety significantly by increasing the timing window for cracking attacks against that key.
Scenario: The product allows for the usage of an expired password(key) without an error, causing entrance from an otherwise unusable password.
Security Impact: bypass protection mechanism, gain privileges, assume identity, integrity
Alexander Reasoning: No system in place to create/switch to new key

Name: Deriect use of unsafe JNI
Author: Jasmine
Description: When a Java application uses the Java Native Interface (JNI) to call code written in another programming language, it can expose the application to weaknesses in that code, even if those weaknesses cannot occur in Java.
Scenario: Native code is unprotected by the security features enforced by the runtime environment, such as strong typing and array bounds checking.
Security Impact: buffer overflow, bypass protection mechanism, input validation
Security Related Design: unprotected native code, jni call error handling, automated static analysis
Alexander root cause analysis: biased review / strong but now wrong / lack of knowledge
Alexander Reasoning: JNI may be useful in other circumstances, but not when calling code in another language

Name: Detection of error condition without action
Author: Jasmine
Description: An attacker could utilize an ignored error condition to place the system in an unexpected state that could lead to the execution of unintended logic and could cause other unintended behavior
Scenario: a caught failed malloc call without any change to resolve the error, an attacker uses this entry point as a weakness.
Security Related Design: error handling, handle exceptions, fuzz testing, fault tolerance, automated static analysis
Alexander root cause analysis: post comlpletion error / lack of security

Name: return of wrong status code
Author: Jasmine
Description: The function returns an incorrect statement or issues an incorrect status code which incorrectly changes the behavior of the parent function.
Scenario: This can lead to unpredictable behavior. If the function is used to make security-critical decisions or provide security-critical information, then the wrong status code can cause the product to assume that an action is safe or correct, even when it is not.
Security Impact: incorrect results, behavioral problems, alter execution logic
Security Related Design: error handling, return values, fuzz testing, wrong status code

Name: the expression is always false
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product contains an expression that will always evaluate to false.
Scenario: a value never updates to a value that would lead to an expression equaling to true.
Security Related Design: coding standards, dead code, automated static analysis

Name: out-of-bounds read
Author: Jasmine
Description: the product reads data past the end, or before the beginning, of the indented buffer.
Scenario: The product executes a memory read that is not intended to be read.
Symptoms: undefined behavior, system crash, reading unauthorized data, security threat
Security Impact: read memory, bypass protection mechanism, system crash, system restart, undefined results, out of bounds read
Security Related Design: input validation, language selection, fuzz testing, automated static analysis, memory management
Alexander root cause analysis: selectivity / strong but now wrong / selectivity

Name: off-by-one-error
Author: Jasmine
Description: a product calculates or uses an incorrect maximum or minimum value that is 1 more, or 1 less, than the correct value.
Scenario: in a for loop that begins either 1 index before or after the true index, or, the ending index condition is either 1 before or 1 after the true index.
Security Impact: off by one error, system crash, system restart, resource consumption, modify memory, bypass protection mechanism

Name: clone() method without super.clone()
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product contains a clone() method that does not call super.clone() to obtain the new object.
Scenario: All implementations of clone() should obtain the new object by calling super.clone(). If a class does not follow this convention, a subclass's clone() method will return an object of the wrong type.

Name: Missing Cryptographic Step
Author: Xander
Description: The product does not implement a required step in a cryptographic algorithm, resulting in weaker encryption than advertised by the algorithm.
Scenario: Missing step/check (such as challenge-response) still allows for functionality but leaves a security hole for exploitation.
Security Impact: bypass protection mechanism, read application data, modify application data, hide activities, encryption

Name: Improper Lock Behavior After Power State Transition
Author: Xander
Description: Register lock bit protection disables changes to system configuration once the bit is set. Some of the protected registers or lock bits become programmable after power state transitions (e.g., Entry and wake from low power sleep modes) causing the system configuration to be changeable.
Scenario: After a power state transition, the lock bit is set to unlocked. Some common weaknesses that can exist in such a protection scheme are that the lock gets cleared, the values of the protected registers get reset, or the lock become programmable.

Name: Exposed Dangerous Method or Function
Author: Xander
Description: The product provides an Applications Programming Interface (API) or similar interface for interaction with external actors, but the interface includes a dangerous method or function that is not properly restricted.
Scenario: This weakness can lead to a wide variety of resultant weaknesses, depending on the behavior of the exposed method. It can apply to any number of technologies and approaches, such as ActiveX controls, Java functions, IOCTLs, and so on.
Security Impact: gain privileges, assume identity, read application data, modify application data, execute unauthorized code
Security Related Design: access control, automated static analysis, attack surface reduction

Name: Undefined Behavior for input to API
Author: Xander
Description: The behavior of this function is undefined unless its control parameter is set to a specific value.

Name: Dead Code
Author: Xander
Description: The product contains dead code, which can never be executed.
Scenario: Dead code is code that can never be executed in a running program. The surrounding code makes it impossible for a section of code to ever be executed.
Security Related Design: bad coding practices, automated static analysis

Name: Incorrect Conversion between Numeric Types
Author: Xander
Description: When converting from one data type to another, such as long to integer, data can be omitted or translated in a way that produces unexpected values. If the resulting values are used in a sensitive context, then dangerous behaviors may occur.

Name: Missing Handler
Author: Xander
Description: A handler is not available or implemented.
Scenario: When an exception is thrown and not caught, the process has given up an opportunity to decide if a given failure or event is worth a change in execution.

Name: Missing Support for Integrity Check
Author: Xander
Description: The product uses a transmission protocol that does not include a mechanism for verifying the integrity of the data during transmission, such as a checksum.
Scenario: If integrity check values or "checksums" are omitted from a protocol, there is no way of determining if data has been corrupted in transmission

Name: Assignment to Variable without use
Author: Xander
Description: The variable's value is assigned but never used, making it a dead store.
Scenario: After the assignment, the variable is either assigned another value or goes out of scope. It is likely that the variable is simply vestigial, but it is also possible that the unused variable points to a bug.
Security Related Design: bad coding practices, automated static analysis
Root cause 1: CM1: forgetting something has been done

Name: Excessive Reliance on Global Variables
Author: Xander
Description: The code is structured in a way that relies too much on using or setting global variables throughout various points in the code, instead of preserving the associated information in a narrower, more local context.
Scenario: This issue makes it more difficult to maintain the product, which indirectly affects security by making it more difficult or time-consuming to find and/or fix vulnerabilities. It also might make it easier to introduce vulnerabilities.
Security Related Design: automated static analysis, bad coding practices

Name: Guessable CAPTCHA
Author: Xander
Description: The product uses a CAPTCHA challenge, but the challenge can be guessed or automatically recognized by a non-human actor.
Scenario: An automated attacker could bypass the intended protection of the CAPTCHA challenge and perform actions at a higher frequency than humanly possible, such as launching spam attacks.

Name: Empty Code Block
Author: Xander
Description: The source code contains a block that does not contain any code, i.e., the block is empty.
Scenario: Empty code blocks can occur in the bodies of conditionals, function or method definitions, exception handlers, etc. While an empty code block might be intentional, it might also indicate incomplete implementation, accidental code deletion, unexpected macro expansion, etc.
Security Related Design: bad coding practices, automated static analysis

Name: Reliance on a single factor in a Security Decision
Author: Xander
Description: A protection mechanism relies exclusively, or to a large extent, on the evaluation of a single condition or the integrity of a single object or entity in order to make a decision about granting access to restricted resources or functionality.
Scenario: Password-only authentication is perhaps the most well-known example of use of a single factor. Anybody who knows a user's password can impersonate that user.
Security Related Design: bad coding practices, secure design principles

Name: Use of hard-coded, Security-relevant Constants
Author: Xander
Description: The product uses hard-coded constants instead of symbolic names for security-critical values, which increases the likelihood of mistakes during code maintenance or security policy change.
Scenario: If the developer does not find all occurrences of the hard-coded constants, an incorrect policy decision may be made if one of the constants is not changed. Making changes to these values will require code changes that may be difficult or impossible once the system is released to the field. In addition, these hard-coded values may become available to attackers if the code is ever disclosed.

Name: Inaccurate Comments
Author: Xander
Description: The source code contains comments that do not accurately describe or explain aspects of the portion of the code with which the comment is associated.
Scenario: When a comment does not accurately reflect the associated code elements, this can introduce confusion to a reviewer (due to inconsistencies) or make it more difficult and less efficient to validate that the code is implementing the intended behavior correctly.

Name: Large Data Table with Excessive Number of Indices
Author: Xander
Description: The product uses a large data table that contains an excessively large number of indices.
Scenario: This issue can make the product perform more slowly. If the relevant code is reachable by an attacker, then this performance problem might introduce a vulnerability.

Name: Signal Handler Race Condition
Author: Xander
Description: Race conditions frequently occur in signal handlers, since signal handlers support asynchronous actions. These race conditions have a variety of root causes and symptoms.
Scenario: Attackers may be able to exploit a signal handler race condition to cause the product state to be corrupted, possibly leading to a denial of service or even code execution.
Security Impact: race condition, modify application data, modify memory, system crash, system restart, execute unauthorized code, gain privileges, assume identity
Security Related Design: automated static analysis, language selection, synchronization, flags

Name: Race Condition within a Thread
Author: Xander
Description: If two threads of execution use a resource simultaneously, there exists the possibility that resources may be used while invalid, in turn making the state of execution undefined.
Scenario: Two threads write to the same file without regard to each other, with one overwriting some data written by the other.
Security Related Design: locking functionality, resource-locking validation checks, flags, signals, automated static analysis

Name: Untrusted Search Path
Author: Xander
Description: The product searches for critical resources using an externally-supplied search path that can point to resources that are not under the product's direct control.
Scenario: This might allow attackers to execute their own programs, access unauthorized data files, or modify configuration in unexpected ways. If the product uses a search path to locate critical resources such as programs, then an attacker could modify that search path to point to a malicious program, which the targeted product would then execute.
Security Impact: gain privileges, assume identity, execute unauthorized code, system crash, system restart, read files, read directories
Security Related Design: attack surface reduction, file handling, authorize actors, black box method, automated static analysis

Name: path equivalence: 'filename' (leading space)
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product accepts path input in the form of leading space (' filedir') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.
Scenario: An input for a pathname is required but no check for the appropriate pathname is checked for.
Security Impact: read files, read directories, modify files, modify directories

Name: improper handling of alternate encoding
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product accepts an alternate form of encoding but does not account for this alternatve form when processing input.
Scenario: input of of UTF-16 is used instead of the common UTF-8 format that the program only accounts for.

Name: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product transmits or stores authentication credentials, but it uses an insecure method that is susceptible to unauthorized interception and/or retrieval.
Scenario: the code confirms that the requesting user typed the same new password twice, it does not confirm that the user requesting the password change is the same user whose password will be changed.
Security Related Design: secure method, cryptography, automated static analysis

Name: Misconfiguration: Data Transmission Without Encryption
Author: Jasmine
Description: Information sent over a network can be compromised while in transit.
Scenario: An attacker may be able to read or modify the contents if the data are sent in plaintext or are weakly encrypted.
Security Impact: read application data, modify application data
Security Related Design: secure transport, secure encryption, system configuration

Name: Path Traversal: '\absolute\pathname\here'
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product accepts input in the form of a backslash absolute path ('\absolute\pathname\here') without appropriate validation, which can allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.
Scenario: A program using a directory path does not treat all inputs as possibly mallicious, therefore, the program does not account for the pathname to start from the root.
Security Impact: read files, read directories, modify files, modify directories

Name: Path Equivalence: '/multiple/trailing/slash//'
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product accepts path input in the form of multiple trailing slash ('/multiple/trailing/slash//') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution.
Scenario: Can allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.
Security Impact: read files, modify files, modify directories

Name: Path Equivalence: 'filedir*' (Wildcard)
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product accepts path input in the form of asterisk wildcard ('filedir*') without appropriate validation, which can lead to ambiguous path resolution and allow an attacker to traverse the file system to unintended locations or access arbitrary files.
Scenario: The program requires input from user, rather than a safe pathname be inputted, an unaccounted for pathname using the wildcard character exposes the system to potentially harmful consequences.
Security Impact: read files, read directories, modify files, modify directories

Name: Path Equivalence: 'fakedir/../realdir/filename'
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product contains protection mechanisms to restrict access to 'realdir/filename', but it constructs pathnames using external input in the form of 'fakedir/../realdir/filename' that are not handled by those mechanisms. This allows attackers to perform unauthorized actions against the targeted file.
Scenario: The program requires a pathname as an input, but inproper directory traversal checks are set up in program, leading to a user traversing to unauthorized locations.
Security Impact: read files, read directories, modify files, modify directories

Name: Struts: Unused Validation Form
Author: Jasmine
Description: An unused validation form indicates that validation logic is not up-to-date. It is easy for developers to forget to update validation logic when they remove or rename action form mappings. One indication that validation logic is not being properly maintained is the presence of an unused validation form.

Name: Buffer Under-read
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product reads from a buffer using buffer access mechanisms such as indexes or pointers that reference memory locations prior to the targeted buffer.
Scenario: A pogrammer writes a pointer to a place where the accessed memory is located before where the stored memory acually is.
Security Impact: read memory, bypass protection mechanism, system crash

Name: Improper Neutralization of Section Delimiters
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as section delimiters when they are sent to a downstream component.
Scenario: One example of a section delimiter is the boundary string in a multipart MIME message. In many cases, doubled line delimiters can serve as a section delimiter.

Name: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product does not verify, or incorrectly verifies, the cryptographic signature for data.
Scenario: An attacker takes advantage of this weakpooint in cryptographic signatures to obtain data that is not theirs.
Security Impact: gain privileges, assume identity, execute unauthorized code

Name: Acceptance of Extraneous Untrusted Data With Trusted Data
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product, when processing trusted data, accepts any untrusted data that is also included with the trusted data, treating the untrusted data as if it were trusted.
Scenario: An attacker packages untrusted data with trusted data to bypass protection mechanism to gain access to and possibly modify sensitive data.
Security Impact: bypass protection mechanism, modify application data

Name: Trapdoor
Author: Jasmine
Description: A trapdoor is a hidden piece of code that responds to a special input, allowing its user access to resources without passing through the normal security enforcement mechanism.
Scenario: An attacker would have unknown capabilities if they are able to bypass through into this trapdoor area of the program.
Security Impact: execute unauthorized code, bypass protection mechanism

Name: EJB Bad Practices: Use of Sockets
Author: Jasmine
Description: The Enterprise JavaBeans specification requires that every bean provider follow a set of programming guidelines designed to ensure that the bean will be portable and behave consistently in any EJB container. In this case, the product violates the following EJB guideline: "An enterprise bean must not attempt to listen on a socket, accept connections on a socket, or use a socket for multicast." The specification justifies this requirement in the following way: "The EJB architecture allows an enterprise bean instance to be a network socket client, but it does not allow it to be a network server. Allowing the instance to become a network server would conflict with the basic function of the enterprise bean-- to serve the EJB clients."
Scenario: a simple stateless Enterprise JavaBean  retrieves stock symbols and stock values. The Enterprise JavaBean creates a socket and listens for and accepts connections from clients on the socket. This violates the EJB specification by attempting to listen on a socket, accepting connections on a socket, or using a socket for multicast.

Name: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
Author: Jasmine
Description: The Secure attribute for sensitive cookies in HTTPS sessions is not set.
Scenario: Attackers could retrieve unsecure cookies from users.

Name: Incorrect Provision of Specified Functionality
Author: Jasmine
Description: When providing functionality to an external party, it is important that the product behaves in accordance with the details specified. When requirements of nuances are not documented, the functionality may produce unintended behaviors for the caller, possibly leading to an exploitable state.
Scenario: an HTTP 404 status code is returned in the event of an IOException encountered in a Java servlet. A 404 code is typically meant to indicate a non-existent resource and would be somewhat misleading in this case.
Security Impact: quality degradation, behavioral problems, exploitable state

Name: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product uses a cookie to store sensitive information, but the cookie is not marked with the HttpOnly flag.
Scenario: cookie is used to store a session ID for a client's interaction with a website. The intention is that the cookie will be sent to the website with each request made by the client. The HttpOnly flag is not set for the cookie. An attacker who can perform XSS could insert malicious script.
Security Impact: gain privileges, assume identity, read application data

Name: Insufficient Visual Distinction of Homoglyphs Presented to User
Author: Jasmine
Description: The product displays information or identifiers to a user, but the display mechanism does not make it easy for the user to distinguish between visually similar or identical glyphs (homoglyphs), which may cause the user to misinterpret a glyph and perform an unintended, insecure action.
Scenario: a simple, trusted URL that a user may frequently access. However, the URL is comprised of Cyrillic characters that look identical to the expected ASCII characters. This results in most users not being able to distinguish between the two and assuming that the above URL is trusted and safe. The "e" is actually the "CYRILLIC SMALL LETTER IE" which is represented in HTML as the character &#x435, while the "a" is actually the "CYRILLIC SMALL LETTER A" which is represented in HTML as the character &#x430. The "p", "c", and "o" are also Cyrillic characters in this example. Viewing the source reveals a URL of "http://www.еxаmрlе.соm". An adversary can utilize this approach to perform an attack such as a phishing attack in order to drive traffic to a malicious website.

Name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
Author: Keagan
Description: Incorrect management of a limited resource allowing them to be maliciously allocated or consumed by attackers. The 3 scenarios that commonly lead to this are:  1.) Lack of throttling for the number of allocated resources 2.) Losing all references to a resource before reaching the shutdown stage  3.) Not closing/returning a resource after processing
Scenario:
The developer may inadequately grasp the scale of their product and how many resources a user could consume, or they may improperly implement the mechanisms that prevent overconsumption of resources.  


Freeing a resource is often times the last step of a task, so it is possible that programmers devote more attention to the steps prior and then forget the last step.

Name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
Author: Keagan
Description: Shared resources can be modified by different code sequences running concurrently.
Scenario: Typically originates from race conditions which occur when a program or part of a program forgets to implement exclusivity and atomicity.
Security Impact: resource consumption, system crash, system restart, read files, read directories, read application data, execute unauthorized code, gain privileges

Name: Use of Hard Coded Credentials
Author: Keagan
Description:
Administrator / High Access login information is hard coded making the program vulnerable to attackers who can discover/steal the login information and utilize admin privileges maliciously.

Program requires credentials to access certain things, such as logging in to access a database, or having a special login for admin access. However, it is possible that these credentials are hard-coded in a configuration file, or they along with their verification checks are hardcoded allowing for attackers to more easily access the information. For example (if password == "adminpassword") kind of verfication check.
Scenario: Developers are probably testing their security inadequately and not considering all the ways that a possible hard-coded authentication could be accessed.  Developers may find it hard to test for these kinds of things themselves, since if they coded the authentication, they may think of course it will be easy for them to find it but for someone else they may expect it to be harder than it actually is.

Name: Out-of-Bounds Write
Author: Keagan
Description: Performing some sort of action on a memory location that is outside of the buffer. Subsequent write operations can then have undefined results including rewriting / corrupting other parts of the memory allowing for attacks.
Scenario: Developers don’t validate that the parameters of the function won’t cause the program to break / overflow the buffer, and don't utilize enough test cases to discover this issue. Adding on to that, sometimes omission of checking for error return values from certain functions calls leads to a buffer overflow via an unexpected parameter value. Nonetheless, a majority of these could be caught, debugged and fixed if there was adequate testing done.
Security Impact: modify memory, execute unauthorized commands, system crash, system restart, unexpected state
Security Related Design: language selection, libraries, frameworks, environment hardening

Name: Improper authentication / verification of user identity
Author: Keagan
Description: Allows for unauthorized attackers to gain possibly sensitive information or execute harmful code. For example, using cookies to store relevant authentication information, and performing actions based on the values in cookies which can be altered by attackers.
Scenario: Developer is unware of the importance of authenticating user, and doesn't place enough emphasis on repeatdely authenticating users before allowing them to access critical information.

Name: Integer Overflow / Wraparound
Author: Keagan
Description: when an integer value is incremented past its max value based depending on data type resulting in a very small or negative number.
Scenario:
More Dominant: Developers do not think it is necessary to check the result of a calculation and assume the result will always be greater after the calculation.

Less Dominant: Humans aren't used to numbers having a max value aside from infinity, so they may not consider the possibility of overflow.
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, modify memory, execute unauthorized commands, bypass protection mechanism
Security Related Design: language selection, libraries, frameworks, input validation, build hardening

Name: Server Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
Author: Keagan
Description: Not sufficiently ensuring that a URL or similar request is being sent to the expected destination. Allows for possible bypassing of access controls and allows attackers to use the server to gain access to confidential content.
Scenario:
Dominant: Developers are not aware of the fact that input can be passed to unexpected hosts or ports and the implications of such behavior.

Less Dominant: Improper limitation of what ports / hosts can receive URLs. Improper input sanitization of URLs is also a major cause of this.
Security Impact: read application data, execute unauthorized code, bypass protection mechanism

Name: Use After Free
Author: Keagan
Description: Reference memory, especially pointers after it has been freed causing unintended program behavior.
Scenario:
More Dominant: The code uses multiple pointers to places in memory and the developers mistakenly reference a similar pointer that has already been freed.

Less Dominant: Developers simply forget that they've already freed something, or may not understand that freeing should be the last step.
Security Impact: modify memory, system crash, system restart, execute unauthorized code
Security Related Design: language selection, attack surface reduction, fuzz testing, automated static analysis

Name: NULL Pointer Dereference
Author: Keagan
Description: Dereferencing a pointer thought to be valid when it is actually null, can cause program crashes / exits.
Scenario:
More Dominant: Developers overlook the common practice to check whether or not pointers are NULL before dereferencing it.

Less Dominant: Improper implementation of synchronization mechanisms
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, execute unauthorized code, read memory, modify memory
Security Related Design: proper locking apis, initialization, automated dynamic analysis, exception checks

Name: Deployment of Wrong Handler
Author: Keagan
Description: The wrong "handler" is assigned to process an object. A handler is commonly a function routine or method tasked with handling a certain type of data or task.
Scenario: A similar task may be successfully handled by one handler, however the task at hand although similar to that one is slightly different, thus making the handler that would typically be logical to use inadequate. For example, in most cases you hammer a nail, and screws are of similar shape/function to nails, however if you hammer a screw, it reduces its strength. More code specific examples include omitting a type check before interpretting an obejct.

Name: Use of Function with Inconsistent Implementations
Author: Keagan
Description: Code utilizes calls to a function that has different implementations from version to version and across different OS. For example, the return values might be different, might handle the input parameters differently or in different order, might not be defined.
Scenario: Developers not having the knowledge that a certain function may behave differently on different systems. Rather, it is more of an operating environment issue, and development tools directly causing the vulnerability, since I would consider function calls to be a development tool. Best example of a similar real-world comparison would be like a word meaning two different things across languages. EX: Kind means nice in English, but it means child in German.

Name: Plaintext Storage of a Password
Author: Keagan
Description:
Password is stored in plaintext in the configuration, properties, or memory of an application, or somewhere equivalently easily accessed. Thus, any attackers who find the password which is easy in this case can gain access to critical information.

Always best practice to at least encrypt passwords in some way, or to not store them within the application they pertain to at the very least.
Scenario:
Stem from the fact that developers don’t test adequately enough to discover this password vulnerability and think it's not plausible for an attacker with no prior information to do so. 

Humans also often opt for convenience over security, and storing a password in plaintext somewhere easily located is a prime example of that, I.e. writing your password down rather than memorizing it.

Name: Incorrect Implementation of Authentication Algorithm
Author: Keagan
Description: The requirements for the product dictate the use of an established authentication algorithm, but the implementation of the algorithm is incorrect.
Scenario:
Dominant Scenario: The description of this vulnerability assumes the security requirements exist, and use of an established authentication algorithm. Thus, it is fair to assume that the developer has knowledge of the algorithm, however the implementation is incorrect or incomplete.

Not as dominant: During testing, they only performed tests that made it appear the algorithm was working correctly.

Name: Improper Removal of Sensitive Information Before Storage or Transfer
Author: Keagan
Description: Critical information within a resource is stored, transferred, or shared before being concealed / removed from the resource. Thus, the information may be available or accessed by possible attackers. Most common resources in which this is relevant include packets, messages, databases, etc.
Scenario:
Deleting or concealing the sensitive information may be the last step before sharing, storing or transferring the resource making it more likely to be forgotten. Kind of like not signing out of a public computer upon finishing your work. 

Not as dominant scenarios: Possible that the sensitive information was made available between a trusted group of people for ease of access reasons, but one member shared the resource outside of the group thinking the information had already been removed. Lastly, there could be a possibility that there was an attempt to remove / conceal the information however it was just done inadequately.
Security Impact: read files, read directories, read application data
Security Related Design: separation of privileges, attack surface reduction, automated static analysis

Name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (‘Open Redirect’)
Author: Keagan
Description: A web application accepts a user-controlled input that specifies a link to an external site, and uses that link in a Redirect. This simplifies phishing attacks. Lastly, this vulnerability stems from attackers feeding web applications abnormal/ harmful inputs.
Scenario:
More dominant: Certain features are not implemented to correctly validate input, or proper preventative measures such as pop up letting the user know they are being redirected to an external URL are not implemented correctly. 

Less dominant: This vulnerability may appear if it is not specified in the requirements of the web application what kind of inputs are acceptable.
Security Related Design: input validation, enforcement by conversion, attack surface reduction, firewall, manual static analysis

Name: Uncontrolled Search Path Element
Author: Keagan
Description: A location in the path used to find or access a resource can be tampered with by attackers.
Scenario: Proper measures to ensure the elements of a path are controlled are not implemented. For example, using the environment variable PATH to find and execute a program, however that variable can be altered by attackers, therefore the program should only accept explicit paths.
Security Impact: execute unauthorized code, execute unauthorized commands
Security Related Design: attack surface reduction, automated static analysis

Name: Inconsistency Between Implementation and Documented Design
Author: Keagan
Description: The implementation of the product is not consistent with the design as described within the relevant documentation.
Scenario: Developers may have interpreted the design documentation in a way that was different than the intended interpretation. Adding on to that, it is possible that during development, new challenges arose and thus the implementation or methods changed however, the design documents were not changed in correspondence. However, if it assumed that there was clear communication, then it would just have to do with incorrect/inaccurate implementation.

Name: Improper Verification of Source of a Communication Channel
Author: Keagan
Description: A communication channel to handle a user request is established, but it does not verify that the request is coming from where it is supposed to be coming from.
Scenario:
May arise from validating user input inadequately or omitting an origin check. 

Less dominant: Additionally, it is possible that this kind of vulnerability is never discovered due to developers not running enough test cases or considering this exception.

Name: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)
Author: Keagan
Description: An incorrect value is produced when a calculation/operation results in a value less than the minimum allowed integer value. Can occur when using signed and unsigned integers.
Scenario: Outside of programming, humans are used to being able to span all negative integers, rather than having a capped maximum value like in programming. Developers should be checking the return value of the calculation to make sure it is within the values they expect, however they omit this or do so inadequately.
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, resource consumption, modify memory, execute unauthorized code, bypass protection mechanism

Name: Creation of Temporary File with Insecure Permissions
Author: Keagan
Description: Opening temporary files without appropriate measures or controls can leave the file, its contents and any function that it impacts vulnerable to attack
Scenario:
Developers inadequately utilize safe functions in creating temporary files, make sure it’s permissions are controlled, and take the proper precautions to ensure the file created won’t alter other parts of the program harmfully. 

More dominant: Developers are just unaware of which functions used in creating a temporary file are unsafe.

Name: Insecure Inherited Permissions
Author: Keagan
Description: Program creates objects that inherit the insecure permissions defined within other part of the program / product.
Scenario:
More dominant: Developers lack knowledge about safer/better practices such as assigning permissions on an object-by-object basis rather than having them inherit permissions, that they may not need or may be unsafe for them to have.

Less Dominant: Requirements may fail to properly establish what permissions are necessary and make sure that they ensure the safety of the program.
Security Impact: read application data, modify application data

Name: Class With Virtual Method w/o a Virtual Destructor
Author: Keagan
Description: Class containing a virtual method does not have a virtual destructor associated with it resulting in undefined behavior that might become vulnerable if discovered my malicious actors.
Scenario:
More dominant: The implementer didn’t know a virtual method needs a virtual destructor.

Less Dominant: Their implementation of the destructor was incorrect or assigning type of method was wrong.

Name: Source Code Element w/o Standard Prologue
Author: Keagan
Description: Source code contains elements such as files that don’t have any documentation explaining them or headers that aren’t standardized for being used within the program/product.
Scenario: A lack of documentation explaining parts of code, how they work, why they work, and any possible drawbacks to be aware of. Thus, if the documentation or explanation was more clearly communicated it would improve the safety of the product, and time taken to find/fix/prevent vulnerabilities. Should be required for code to have proper comments, especially if they aren’t commonly used within a given project, etc. however that is not specified.

Name: Least Privledge Violation
Author: Keagan
Description: Elevated privledge levels granted to perform certain operations aren't dropped immediatly after operation is done.
Scenario: The developer may forget to restrict the privledges once the operations that needed the privledges have been performed or may do them at a later time rather than immediatly, leaving the app open to malicious attacks.
Security Impact: gain privileges, read application data, read files, read directories

Name: Use of Same Variable for Multiple Purposes
Author: Keagan
Description: Code element in which same variable is used for multiple purposes. May make code unsafe, harder to read, and thus more susceptible to attacks.
Scenario: Developer may name multiple variables the same and thus access one when they really mean to access the other, etc. Programmer may also think that a certain variable hasn't been used yet or doesn't need the data it's storing anymore and mistakenly overwrite it.

Name: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
Author: Keagan
Description: Product allocates a resource without having any limits on the size or number of resources allocated allowing for the possibility of resources to be overconsumed.
Scenario:
The amount of resources avaliable, and or how many should be allocated to certain users / certain tasks is not limited or specified in the documentation.

More dominant: Developers may not consider the scale of their work, and what would happen if large amount of users use the app or user inputs certain very large inputs requiring lots of resources. Ex: Not capping number of connections in client-server program

Name: Insufficient Isolation of System-Dependent Functions
Author: Keagan
Description: "The product or code does not isolate system-dependent functionality into separate standalone modules. " Indirectly affects security as it may make vulnerabilities harder to find in code, or allow them to spread more easily.
Scenario:
More dominant: Developers may have challenges grasping the functionality of what they are building and how to break it down into smaller compartamentalized parts. 

Clear direction may also be lacked in the specification or documentation for the product making it easier to confuse how to isolate functional components.

Name: Critcal Data Element Declared Public
Author: Keagan
Description: "The product declares a critical variable, field, or member to be public when intended security policy requires it to be private. "
Scenario: Developers may engage in bad coding practices such as declaring all variables as public for testing or development reasons, but forget to change them back.

Name: Incorrect or Absent Check of Function Return Value
Author: Keagan
Description: Function return value isn't checked properly leaving possible errors or exceptions undetected.
Scenario: Developers may not understand that certain functions return, what their return values mean, or may assume the function will always return the same thing so there is no reason to check the value. Programmers may also forget completly to check a return value.
Security Impact: unexpected state, system crash, system restart
Security Related Design: language selection, function design, automated static analysis

Name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
Author: Keagan
Description: Product contains code that utilizes SQL with externally / user influenced input but it does not handle special elements that could modify the SQL command before being executed.
Scenario: Developer may utilize unsafe SQL commands (unparameterized), or does not properly validate input that may affect the SQL commands being run. Testing done on the product does not consider potentially harmful inputs or their affects.
Security Impact: execute unauthorized code, read application data, gain privileges, bypass protection mechanism, modify application data
Security Related Design: libraries, frameworks, parameterization, environment hardening, input validation, firewall, output validation

Name: Direct Request 'Forced Browsing'
Author: Keagan
Description: Appropriate authorization not sufficiently enforced for restricted URLs, scripts, or files. Essentially pages or files that should only be accessed by users with certain privledges but can be accessed by inputting a certain URL directly.
Scenario: Developers may assume certain resources are only reachable a certain way and so only do authorization checks at a few points rather than all possible points.
Security Impact: read application data, execute unauthorized code, gain privileges

Name: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
Author: Keagan
Description: Product generates an error message either via the source code or an external handler that reveals directly sensitive information or information for futher attacks. Catching exceptions and printing the error is often considered best practice and useful for debugging, however it may sometimes reveal too much information.
Scenario:
Developers may forget to alter the error messages before releasing the product. 

More dominant: Developers conduct testing, and look at these error messages but don't consider how the information could be used for further attacks, possibly because they lack knowledge about attacks.
Security Related Design: attack surface reduction, compilation hardening, default error pages,

Name: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')
Author: Keagan
Description: Product uses some code that is dependent / affected by external inputs without making sure that the input won't modify the manner in which the code is run.
Scenario: Stems from developers not adequately sanitizing the external input that will be used in their code, and using particularly dangerous / exploitable functions such as eval ... However, these should be caught in the review phase if the testing team ran adequate tests and considered cases in which users input code directly.
Security Impact: bypass protection mechanism, gain privileges, execute unauthorized code

Name: Origin Validation Error
Author: Keagan
Description: "The product does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid. "
Scenario: Developer does not correctly implement validation methods necessary, maybe because they are unaware of when the data they are communicating could potentially be vulnerable.

Name: Operator Precedence Logic Error
Author: Keagan
Description: "The product uses an expression in which operator precedence causes incorrect logic to be used."
Scenario:
Most often caused by developers just forgetting or misplacing parenthesis when doing critical calculations. 

Less dominant: Variation in operator precedence across programming languages attributing to programmers implementing incorrect logic in one language that may be correct if written in another language

Name: Active Debug Code
Author: Keagan
Description: Product is deployed / released while debugging code is still active. This debugging code allows for attackers to access sensitive information or reach certain areas they shouldn't normally
Scenario:
Most likely stems from the team running tests with the debug code to ensure everything is working, however they never run testing from the users point of view. 

More dominant: Removing debug code may be the last step before releasing the product, and it is possible some code slips through the cracks / the team forgets to remove the code
Security Impact: bypass protection mechanism, read application data, gain privileges

Name: Improper Authorization in Handler for Custom URL Scheme
Author: Keagan
Description: Vulnerability only present in Mobile Development: A URL scheme is what defines which URLs direct to your app and the context in which they load the app. Product uses a handler for a URL scheme but allows the handler to be accessed via URL scheme by unauthorized people. Also the URL scheme is used for critical tasks such as modifying files, or can be used to alter the code.
Scenario: Seems like URL schemes while useful for referencing resources, and communicating with apps are inherently flawed and unsafe. However, there are stilll workarounds like validating the URL parameters, and limiting actions that can be performed via the handler for URL schemes, that developers should be aware of.

Name: Improper Isolation or Compartmentalization
Author: Keagan
Description: "The product does not properly compartmentalize or isolate functionality, processes, or resources that require different privilege levels, rights, or permissions"
Scenario:
All of those things mentioned in the description should be clearly defined within the security requirements so that implementation is clear and easy to break down into parts. 

More dominant: A product may be overlycomplex, making it harder for developers to break down the functionality into multiple parts, especially if they all work closely together.

Name: Excessive Use of Unconditional Branching
Author: Theo
Description: The code uses too many unconditional branches (such as "goto").
Scenario: The developer uses multiple goto statements to avoid refactoring code. This might occur in a function with preexisting control flow complexity, e.g. nested loops.

Name: Use of a Non-reentrant Function in a Concurrent Context
Author: Theo
Description: The program calls a non-reentrant function in a concurrent context in which a competing code sequence (e.g. thread or signal handler) may have an opportunity to call the same function or otherwise influence its state.
Scenario: The developer does not know a particular function is non-reentrant or does not have proper knowledge of how re-entrant functions work.

Name: Authentication Bypass by Alternate Name
Author: Theo
Description: The program performs authentication based on the name of a resource being accessed, or the name of the actor performing the access, but it does not properly check all possible names for that resource or actor.
Scenario: URLs can be encoded in multiple ways (e.g. \ vs %5C), and if authentication is triggered by comparing the URL string, it may not account for all variations.

Name: Incomplete List of Disallowed Inputs
Author: Theo
Description: Developers often try to protect their products against malicious input by performing tests against inputs that are known to be bad, such as special characters that can invoke new commands. However, such lists often only account for the most well-known bad inputs. Attackers may be able to find other malicious inputs that were not expected by the developer, allowing them to bypass the intended protection mechanism.
Scenario: Developer attempts to sanitize input (e.g. removing all occurences of “script”), but fails to account for alternate spellings

Name: Incorrect Block Delimitation
Author: Theo
Description: In some languages, braces (or other delimiters) are optional for blocks. When the delimiter is omitted, it is possible to insert a logic error in which a statement is thought to be in a block but is not. In some cases, the logic error can have security implications.
Scenario:
A developer types the code:
If (condition)
Do_X()
Do_Y()

Assuming Do_Y() is executed conditionally, but it is not.

Name: Insufficient Entropy
Author: Theo
Description: The program uses an algorithm or scheme that produces insufficient entropy, leaving patterns or clusters of values that are more likely to occur than others.
Scenario: A developer uses a pseudo random number generator to create a unique ID for users, instead of a cryptographically secure one, giving a fair amount of predictive power to an attacker.

Name: Reliance on Security Through Obscurity
Author: Theo
Description: The program uses a protection mechanism whose strength depends heavily on its obscurity, such that knowledge of its algorithms or key data is sufficient to defeat the mechanism.
Scenario: The reliance on "security through obscurity" can produce resultant weaknesses if an attacker is able to reverse engineer the inner workings of the mechanism. Note that obscurity can be one small part of defense in depth, since it can create more work for an attacker; however, it is a significant risk if used as the primary means of protection.
Security Related Design: bad coding practices, secure design principles

Name: Missing Initialization of Resource
Author: Theo
Description: The program does not initialize a critical resource.
Scenario: Many resources require initialization before they can be properly used. If a resource is not initialized, it could contain unpredictable or expired data, or it could be initialized to defaults that are invalid. This can have security implications when the resource is expected to have certain properties or values.
Security Impact: read memory, system crash, system restart

Name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
Author: Theo
Description: The program transmits sensitive or security-critical data in cleartext in a communication channel that can be sniffed by unauthorized actors.
Scenario: The developer transmits user credentials using HTTP.
Security Impact: read application data, modify files, modify directories

Name: Excessive Use of Self-Modifying Code
Author: Theo
Description: This issue makes it more difficult to understand or maintain the product, which indirectly affects security by making it more difficult or time-consuming to find and/or fix vulnerabilities. It also might make it easier to introduce vulnerabilities.
Scenario:
Self-modifying code may be used to extend functionality of existing code, or to condense variable type of functionality into a smaller design.
The alternative would be refactoring the code or using higher level design patterns like polymorphism.

Name: Use of Less Trusted Source
Author: Theo
Description: The program has two different sources of the same data or information, but it uses the source that has less support for verification, is less trusted, or is less resistant to attack.
Scenario: The developer checks the IP address specified by the user instead of the packet header, allowing for IP spoofing.

Name: Use of Externally-Controlled Input to Select Classes or Code
Author: Theo
Description: The program uses external input with reflection to select which classes or code to use, but it does not sufficiently prevent the input from selecting improper classes or code.
Scenario: The developer has a function that constructs a class instance from a classname string, not realizing this can be exploited by an attacker.

Name: Improper Validation of Integrity Check Value
Author: Theo
Description: The program does not validate or incorrectly validates the integrity check values or "checksums" of a message. This may prevent it from detecting if the data has been modified or corrupted in transmission.
Scenario: The developer implements the checksum verification algorithm incorrectly.

Name: Overly Restrictive Regular Expression
Author: Theo
Description: A regular expression is overly restrictive, which prevents dangerous values from being detected.
Scenario: The developer considers a specific input that should be invalidated but does not exhaustively consider all possibilities.

Name: Return of Stack Variable Address
Author: Theo
Description: A function returns the address of a stack variable, which will cause unintended program behavior, typically in the form of a crash.
Scenario: Mistaken for a different type of variable e.g. (pointer).
Security Impact: read memory, modify memory, execute unauthorized code, system crash, system restart

Name: Omission of Security-relevant Information
Author: Theo
Description: The program does not record or display information that would be important for identifying the source or nature of an attack, or determining if an action is safe.
Scenario:
Incomplete understanding of/no conception of task.
Developer is unaware what information to log for security analysis or believes existing reporting is sufficient.

Name: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password
Author: Theo
Description: The program contains a mechanism for users to recover or change their passwords without knowing the original password, but the mechanism is weak.
Scenario:
Incomplete understanding of goal.
Developer prioritizes the implementation of the account recovery process over the security mechanisms.

Name: Missing Lock Check
Author: Theo
Description: The program does not check to see if a lock is present before performing sensitive operations on a resource.
Scenario: Mistaking atomic variable for non-atomic variable (unlikely). Developer has an incomplete understanding of the rule involved for atomic variable accesses (more likely).
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, modify application data

Name: Unchecked Input for Loop Condition
Author: Theo
Description: The program does not properly check inputs that are used for loop conditions, potentially leading to a denial of service or other consequences because of excessive looping.
Scenario: The developer believes that all attack scenarios have been addressed.

Name: Improper Null Termination
Author: Theo
Description: The program does not terminate or incorrectly terminates a string or array with a null character or equivalent terminator.
Scenario: When constructing a string, the developer forgets the last step to append a null character.
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, alter execution logic, read memory, modify memory

Name: Expired Pointer Dereference
Author: Theo
Description: The program dereferences a pointer that contains a location for memory that was previously valid, but is no longer valid.
Scenario:
The developer is not aware of the location where the memory becomes invalidated.
The developer uses existing methods to manipulate the pointer, but these no longer work in the new context.
Security Impact: read memory, system crash, system restart, execute unauthorized code

Name: Improper Enforcement of Behavioral Workflow
Author: Theo
Description: The program supports a session in which more than one behavior must be performed by an actor, but it does not properly ensure that the actor performs the behaviors in the required sequence.
Scenario: The developer does not understand the significance of the ordering of certain logic.

Name: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input before it is placed in output that is used as a web page that is served to other users.
Scenario: This happens when a developer implementing some form of content display fails to validate that external/user-generated content to be safe.
Security Impact: bypass protection mechanism, execute unauthorized code, read application data
Security Related Design: output encoding, input validation, firewall, environment hardening

Name: Incorrect Usage of Seeds in Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
Author: Marvin
Description: The product uses a Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG) but does not correctly manage seeds.
Scenario: This happens when a developer does not know the importance of using a secure source to generate seeds. A common example is using the time to generate the seed, which makes it easy to guess.

Name: Incorrect Default Permissions
Author: Marvin
Description: Permissions are incorrectly set so that unauthorized users can access files/data.
Scenario: This is possibly caused by complexity within the rules used to manage permissions among many levels of user groups and such.
Security Impact: read application data, modify application data,

Name: Incomplete Documentation of Program Execution
Author: Marvin
Description: The document does not fully define all mechanisms that are used to control or influence how product-specific programs are executed.
Scenario: This describes when the code requirements are incomplete.

Name: Improper Resource Locking
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not lock or does not correctly lock a resource when the product must have exclusive access to the resource.
Scenario: This is caused when one does not know that a shared resource/variable must be synchronized to be used. In the example of a bank balance, it would be necessary to make sure the balance is up to date before a withdraw is authorized, for example.
Security Related Design: improper locking, resource locking, automated static analysis

Name: Collapse of Data into Unsafe Value
Author: Marvin
Description: The product filters data in a way that causes it to be reduced or "collapsed" into an unsafe value that violates an expected security property.
Scenario: Dev overlooks the need to validate path names

Name: Missing Password Field Masking
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not mask passwords during entry, increasing the potential for attackers to observe and capture passwords.
Scenario: This happens when implementers do not know you're supposed to use type="password".

Name: Incorrect Calculation of Buffer Size
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not correctly calculate the size to be used when allocating a buffer, which could lead to a buffer overflow.
Scenario: This could occur when a developer does not fully grasp all the variables involved in their calculations.
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, execute unauthorized code, read memory, modify memory
Security Related Design: protect credentials, user interface security issues

Name: Improperly Implemented Security Check for Standard
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not implement or incorrectly implements one or more security-relevant checks as specified by the design of a standardized algorithm, protocol, or technique.
Scenario: This happens when a developer does not implement or makes a mistake when implementing a requirement.
Security Related Design: input validation, environment hardening, libraries, frameworks, memory buffer errors, automated static analysis

Name: Reliance on Cookies without Validation and Integrity Checking
Author: Marvin
Description: The product relies on the existence or values of cookies when performing security-critical operations, but it does not properly ensure that the setting is valid for the associated user.
Scenario: Dev doesn't know cookies can be edited

Name: Reliance on Package-level Scope
Author: Marvin
Description: Java packages are not inherently closed; therefore, relying on them for code security is not a good practice.
Scenario: Dev doesn't know packages aren't secure
Security Impact: read application data, modify application data

Name: Use of Weak Hash
Author: Marvin
Description: The product uses an algorithm that produces a digest (output value) that does not meet security expectations for a hash function that allows an adversary to reasonably determine the original input (preimage attack), find another input that can produce the same hash (2nd preimage attack), or find multiple inputs that evaluate to the same hash (birthday attack).
Scenario: Dev doesn't know about the need to salt passwords/use a secure algorithm.

Name: Execution After Redirect (EAR)
Author: Marvin
Description: The web application sends a redirect to another location, but instead of exiting, it executes additional code.
Scenario: Dev doesn't know redirects can be blocked by the user.
Security Impact: alter execution logic, execute unauthorized code

Name: Explicit Call to Finalize()
Author: Marvin
Description: The product makes an explicit call to the finalize() method from outside the finalizer.
Scenario: Dev doesn't know finalize() shouldn't be used

Name: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
Author: Marvin
Description: The product performs operations on a memory buffer, but it can read from or write to a memory location that is outside of the intended boundary of the buffer.
Scenario: Dev overlooks the need to check if variables used in accessing memory are in bounds

Name: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')
Author: Marvin
Description: The product copies an input buffer to an output buffer without verifying that the size of the input buffer is less than the size of the output buffer, leading to a buffer overflow.
Scenario: Dev might validate user input in some ways but forget to check if it's too long
Security Impact: modify memory, system crash, system exit, system restart, resource consumption

Name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements
Author: Marvin
Description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could be interpreted as control elements or syntactic markers when they are sent to a downstream component.
Scenario: Dev validates user input but forgets to check special characters
Security Related Design: environment hardening, input validation, memory management

Name: Race Condition Enabling Link Following
Author: Marvin
Description: The product checks the status of a file or directory before accessing it, which produces a race condition in which the file can be replaced with a link before the access is performed, causing the product to access the wrong file.
Scenario: This issue happens when the developers do not know about the potential vulnerability in files being changed to file links after a check to bypass security measures.
Security Impact: read files, modify files, modify directories

Name: Password Aging with Long Expiration
Author: Marvin
Description: The product supports password aging, but the expiration period is too long.
Scenario: Outdated CWE from when password ageing was common.
Security Related Design: automated static analysis, race conditions, c secure coding standards

Name: Incorrect Calculation of Multi-Byte String Length
Author: Rylan
Description: The product does not correctly calculate the length of strings that can contain wide or multi-byte characters.
Scenario: A program may require the length of a String variable  i.e. for memory allocation purposes. A developer may mistakenly calculate the number of characters in the string, using a method such as strlen() in C, instead of finding the size of the string in bytes.
Security Impact: execute unauthorized code, system crash, system exit, system restart, resource consumption

Name: Observable Response Discrepancy
Author: Rylan
Description: The product provides different responses to incoming requests in a way that reveals internal state information to an unauthorized actor outside of the intended control sphere.
Scenario:
During the documentation stage, failing to precisely clarify what information is to be hidden or revealed from the program will likely lead to a vulnerability.

When reviewing a program that accepts and responds to requests, such as a user login page, the developer may fail to consider that informing whether an invalid password or username was entered may support a bad actor in brute forcing credentials.
Security Impact: read application data, bypass protection mechanism
Security Related Design: input validation, libraries, frameworks, string errors

Name: Integer Coercion Error
Author: Rylan
Description: Integer coercion refers to a set of flaws pertaining to the type casting, extension, or truncation of primitive data types.
Scenario: A developer may mistakenly set a variable as a unsigned int instead of an int, or vice versa, due to the similarity between the two data types.
Security Impact: resource consumption, system crash, system restart, execute unauthorized code, incorrect results

Name: Reliance on Data/Memory Layout
Author: Rylan
Description: The product makes invalid assumptions about how protocol data or memory is organized at a lower level, resulting in unintended program behavior.
Scenario: Developers operating in a lower level programming language may make assumptions about how memory is organized, such as two variables being one byte apart. Implementing code with these assumptions may lead to erroneous code that can be exploited.

Name: Buffer Access with Incorrect Length Value
Author: Rylan
Description: Rylan
Scenario:
A developer implementing a method that accepts and saves user input, such as an IP address, may make assumptions about the size of the input. If they fail to consider cases where the input exceeds the assumed size in the review size, the code may be exploitable.

When writing into a buffer, a programmer may mistake the size of the source buffer for the destination buffer. Writing into the destination buffer with the size of the source may lead to a vulnerability.
Security Related Design: encapsulation, grammar, memory management, fuzz testing, automated dynamic analysis

Name: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access
Author: Rylan
Description: The product attempts to access a file based on the filename, but it does not properly prevent that filename from identifying a link or shortcut that resolves to an unintended resource.
Scenario:
A developer who implemented software that access files may not have considered cases where certain provided links or shortcuts can access other resources in the system during the review stage.

During the documentation stage, failing to clarify clearly how user input needs to be verified when accepting filenames can lead to a vulnerability.
Security Impact: read files, read directories, bypass protection mechanism, execute unauthorized code

Name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
Author: Rylan
Description: The product specifies permissions for a security-critical resource in a way that allows that resource to be read or modified by unintended actors.
Scenario:
During the documentation stage, failing to clearly state the appropriate permissions for critical resources, such as user data, may lead to a vulnerability.

While reviewing their code, a developer may fail to realize that certain permissions given to a resource could be abused by an attacker
Security Impact: read application data, read files, gain privileges, modify application data

Name: Improper Validation of Array Index
Author: Rylan
Description: The product uses untrusted input when calculating or using an array index, but the product does not validate or incorrectly validates the index to ensure the index references a valid position within the array.
Scenario:
A developer reviewing software that writes or access an array using a value gained from user input may fail to consider cases where the user inputs an index outside of the array, potentially leading to a vulnerability.
If a developer is tasked with implementing a method that accesses an array as well as verifying the index provided by a user is valid, the programmer may focus more on the array method, as it is required for functionality of the program, omitting the task of verifying the input
Security Related Design: authorize actors, encapsulation, automated static analysis

Name: Use of resources with Duplicate Identifier
Author: Rylan
Description: The product uses multiple resources that can have the same identifier, in a context in which unique identifiers are required.
Scenario: If no standard convention is established on a large software project, developer may coincidently name certain resources within the software identical names, potentially leading to vulnerabilities.

Name: Compilation with Insufficient Warnings or Errors
Author: Rylan
Description: The code is compiled without sufficient warnings enabled, which may prevent the detection of subtle bugs or quality issues.
Scenario:
Software developers not fully utilizing the compilers provided by not enabling warning flags may lead to vulnerabilities in the code.
Developers utilizing new compilers or technologies may not be aware of certain warnings that must be enable to correctly review code.
Security Related Design: neutralization, resource management errors, unique identifiers

Name: Reliance on Machine-Dependent Data Representation
Author: Rylan
Description: The code uses a data representation that relies on low-level data representation or constructs that may vary across different processors, physical machines, OSes, or other physical components.
Scenario: Developers may be unaware that certain aspects of their system are system dependent, which may lead to vulnerabilities across different platforms utilizing the software.
Security Related Design: improper adherence to coding standards, bad coding practices

Name: Privilege Defined with Unsafe Actions
Author: Rylan
Description: A particular privilege, role, capability, or right can be used to perform unsafe actions that were not intended, even when it is assigned to the correct entity.
Scenario: During the documentation stage, failing to clearly define the privileges of all roles in a system can lead to certain roles having inappropriate and unsafe capabilities.
Security Related Design: bad coding practices, undefined behavior, encapsulation

Name: The product divides a value by zero
Author: Rylan
Description: The product divides a value by zero.
Scenario: A developer lacking knowledge in mathematics, or a programs character set may divide a variable by zero, not realizing it could lead to an exception.
Security Related Design: unauthorized actors, privilege management, automated static analysis, fuzz testing

Name: Incorrect Comparison
Author: Rylan
Description: The product compares two entities in a security-relevant context, but the comparison is incorrect, which may lead to resultant weaknesses.
