Scenario: If a developer is tasked with creating a protection mechanism that relies on  a set of inputs, as well as verifying the received inputs, the mechanism may appear to be more noteworthy, potentially omitting the input validation, leading to a potential vulnerability.

Name: Only Filtering Special Element at a Specified Location
Author: Rylan
Description: The product receives data from an upstream component, but only accounts for special elements at a specified location, thereby missing remaining special elements that may exist before sending it to a downstream component.
Scenario: A developer reviewing a product that filters out special elements may mistakenly conclude that a special element will only appear at a certain point in the input, and believe all cases have been accounted for, leading to a potential vulnerability.

Name: Insertion of Sensitive Information Into Sent Data
Author: Rylan
Description: The code transmits data to another actor, but a portion of the data includes sensitive information that should not be accessible to that actor.
Scenario:
During documentation, failing to ensure least privilege principles among modules and messages may result in developers disclosing this information the wrong actor.
A module may require an API call such as "HTTP post" to ensure sensitive information is not accessible by users, however a developer may use another similar method such as "HTTP get" which has worked successfully in the past for similar requirements and is similar to post, potentially leading to leaked data to unintended actors
Security Impact: read files, read memory, read application data

Name: Reliance on Reverse DNS Resolution for a Security-Critical Action
Author: Rylan
Description: The product performs reverse DNS resolution on an IP address to obtain the hostname and make a security decision, but it does not properly ensure that the IP address is truly associated with the hostname.
Scenario: A developer reviewing a feature that uses reverse DNS resolution to obtain a hostname may fail to consider a case in which an attacker poisons the DNS cache, leading to a potential attack.

Name: Protection Mechanism Failure
Author: Rylan
Description: The product does not use or incorrectly uses a protection mechanism that provides sufficient defense against directed attacks against the product.
Scenario:
Developers may not be aware or consider use of a provided security tool, potentially leading to a vulnerability that could’ve been easily mitigated.
Developers may use a security tool such as encryption to a believed expected standard, however, it may not meet modern day security requirements, such as level of encryption.

Name: Cross-Site Request Forgery
Author: Rylan
Description: The web application does not, or can not, sufficiently verify whether a well-formed, valid, consistent request was intentionally provided by the user who submitted the request.
Scenario:
A lack a security requirements relating to request verification will likely lead to a vulnerability. 
 
Developers may review a program receiving requests and overlook the condition to verify the request, potentially leading to a vulnerability.
Security Impact: gain privileges, bypass security mechanism, system crash, system restart

Name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
Author: Rylan
Description: The product allows the attacker to upload or transfer files of dangerous types that can be automatically processed within the product's environment.
Scenario:
A lack a security requirements relating to file verification will likely lead to a vulnerability. 
Developers may review a program receiving file uploads and overlook the condition to verify the file type, potentially leading to a vulnerability.
Security Impact: execute unauthorized code, execute unauthorized commands
Security Related Design: libraries, frameworks, cross-site scripting, input validation

Name: Missing Authorization
Author: Rylan
Description: The product does not perform an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.
Scenario:
A lack a security requirements relating to actor authorization will likely lead to a vulnerability. 
Developers may review a program allowing users to access resources and overlook the condition to verify the authority of the user, potentially leading to a vulnerability.
Security Impact: gain privileges, bypass protection mechanism, system crash, system restart, read application data
Security Related Design: input validation, environment hardening, authorize actors

Name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
Author: Rylan
Description: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently verifying that the resulting data will be valid.
Scenario:
A lack a security requirements relating to verifying deserialized data will likely lead to a vulnerability. 
Developers may review a program allowing sources to submit serialized data to the software and overlook the condition to verify the resulting deserialized data, potentially leading to a vulnerability.
Security Impact: modify application data, unexpected state, resource consumption

Name: OS Command Injection
Author: Rylan
Description: The product constructs all or part of an OS command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements that could modify the intended OS command when it is sent to a downstream component.
Scenario:
A lack a security requirements relating to neutralizing escape characters will likely lead to a vulnerability. 
Developers may review a program constructing OS commands using user input and overlook the condition to neutralize escape characters potentially leading to a vulnerability.
Security Impact: execute unauthorized code, system crash, system restart, read files, modify files, read application data
Security Related Design: firewall, cryptography, input validation, error handling

Name: Use of Cache Containing Sensitive Information
Author: Theo
Description: The code uses a cache that contains sensitive information, but the cache can be read by an actor outside of the intended control sphere.
Scenario: Websites often use caches to temporarily store information provided by the user. If this information is not securely stored, vulnerabilities in the web browser could allow attackers to access this sensitive information.
Security Related Design: libraries, frameworks, input validation, environment hardening, neutralization

Name: Execution with Unnecessary Privileges
Author: Theo
Description: The program performs an operation at a privilege level that is higher than the minimum level required, which creates new weaknesses or amplifies the consequences of other weaknesses.
Scenario:
One common example of unnecessary privilege escalation is on Linux platforms, i.e. running as root.

It's also common for unnecessary privileges to be requested from an API, for example requesting mutable access to an object versus a read-only getter function.
Security Impact: gain privileges, execute unauthorized code, read application data, system crash, system restart

Name: Reachable Assertion
Author: Theo
Description: The program contains an assert() or similar statement that can be triggered by an attacker, which leads to an application exit or other behavioe that is more severe than necessary.
Scenario: Assertions are typically used during development of a program, but can cause unintended behavior if triggered in a production environment. Developers must make sure to exhaustively remove all leftover assertion functions.
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, off by one error
Security Related Design: environment hardening, attack surface reduction, input validation, separation of privileges

Name: Class with Excessively Deep Inheritance
Author: Theo
Description: A class has an inheritance level that is too high, i.e., it has a large number of parent classes.
Scenario: Excessive inheritance is a poor coding practice and serves as a distraction for developers, increasing the odds that vulnerabilities are introduced.

Name: Source Code File with Excessive Number of Lines of Code
Author: Theo
Description: A source code file has too many lines of code.
Scenario: Excessively large source code files can be difficult to maintain. This unnecessary complexity makes it more difficult for developers to maintain the code and increases the odds of vulnerabilities being introduced and missed.

Name: Unchecked Return Value
Author: Theo
Description: The program does not check the return value from a method or function, which can prevent it from detecting unexpected states and conditions.
Scenario: A developer may choose not to read the return value of a function, potentially missing the report of an error. The developer may believe that the error does not affect the operation of the program or that the odds of the error are negligble, but this is not a safe practice.
Security Impact: unexpected state, system crash, system restart

Name: Use of Incorrect Operator
Author: Theo
Description: The program accidentally uses the wrong operator, which changes the logic in security-relevant ways.
Scenario: This defect may arise due to a typo. For example, = (single equals) and == (double equals) have different behaviors but a slip by the developer may result in the wrong one being typed.
Security Related Design: return values, null pointer dereference, error handling

Name: Deadlock
Author: Theo
Description: The program contains multiple threads or executable segments that are waiting for each other to release a necessary lock, resulting in deadlock.
Scenario: Developers should prepare for deadlock when designing multithreaded programs. Deadlock may occur when assumptions are made about the status of a resource.
Security Impact: resource consumption, system crash, system restart

Name: Use of Low-Level Functionality
Author: Theo
Description: The program uses low-level functionality that is explicitly prohibited by the framework or specification under which the product is supposed to operate.
Scenario: The developer may not be aware of certain features offered by the framework, and attempts to implement the functionality themselves. Additionally, they may feel confident in their ability to circumvent warnings provided by the framework about unsafe features.

Name: Use of Redundant Code
Author: Theo
Description: The program has multiple functions, methods, procedures, macros, etc. that contain the same code.
Scenario: Redundant code can clutter the development repository and lead to complexity errors. Code redundancy emerges from the development phase and can be resolved during review passes. It is important for developers to establish a routine phase of code refactoring.

Name: Parent Class without Virtual Destructor Method
Author: Theo
Description: A parent class contains one or more child classes, but the parent class does not have a virtual destructor method.
Scenario: In C++, it is undefined behavior to derive from a class that does not have a virtual destructor. Classes with no children do not have this requirement. The developer may not be aware of this feature of the programming language.

Name: Creation of Class Instance within a Static Code Block
Author: Theo
Description: A static code block creates an instance of a class.
Scenario: This issue can make the product perform more slowly by performing initialization before it is needed. If the relevant code is reachable by an attacker, then this performance problem might introduce a vulnerability.

Name: Double-Checked Locking
Author: Theo
Description: The program uses double-checked locking to access a resource in place of synchronization mechanisms and the locking is insufficient.
Scenario: In some languages, atomic synchronization can be substituted for "double-checked locking". However, this functionality is not supported in all languages and could result in a race condition.

Name: Excessively Deep Nesting
Author: Theo
Description: The code contains a callable or other code grouping in which the nesting / branching is too deep.
Scenario: Excessive nesting is often a symptom of a lack of code refactoring. Developers should move deeply nested code into a new function to reduce code complexity.
Security Related Design: resource locking, resource management, automated static analysis

Name: Use of Externally-Controlled Format String
Author: Rylan
Description: During execution of non-reentrant code, the product performs a call that unintentionally produces a nested invocation of the non-reentrant code.
Scenario: A developer may be required to review a heavily nested function, and fail to consider global dependencies hidden beneath a high number of layers.
Security Impact: read memory, modify memory, execute unauthorized code

Name: Privilege Chaining
Author: Rylan
Description: Two distinct privileges, roles, capabilities, or rights can be combined in a way that allows an entity to perform unsafe actions that would not be allowed without that combination.
Scenario: A developer may be required to review distinct roles/groups for security, but fail to consider the interactions they have with eachother.

Name: Incorrect Pointer Scaling
Author: Rylan
Description: In C and C++, one may often accidentally refer to the wrong memory due to the semantics of when math operations are implicitly scaled.
Scenario: A developer may be required to increment a pointer value by one byte, and assume it should increment by one. In many cases, this will increment by the size of one data type, as opposed to one byte.
Security Related Design: separation of privileges, environment hardening, authorize actors

Name: Class Instance Self Destruction Control Element
Author: Rylan
Description: The code contains a class instance that calls the method or function to delete or destroy itself.
Scenario: It is bad practice for a class to delete itself, however a developer may be unaware of this rule and implement it in their code.

Name: Reflection Attack in an Authentication Protocol
Author: Rylan
Description: Simple authentication protocols are subject to reflection attacks if a malicious user can use the target machine to impersonate a trusted user.
Scenario: A developer reviewing a secure authentication scheme may verify its integrity, but fail to consider a case where a malicuous user reflects previously requested hashes to gain authentication.

Name: Use of Single-factor Authentication
Author: Rylan
Description: The use of single-factor authentication can lead to unnecessary risk of compromise when compared with the benefits of a dual-factor authentication scheme.
Scenario: Developers may have designed software in the past utilizing single factor authentication, failing to realize the robust and numerous attacks it is susceptible to in modern times.

Name: Storing Passwords in a Recoverable Format
Author: Rylan
Description: The storage of passwords in a recoverable format makes them subject to password reuse attacks by malicious users.
Scenario: Developers may believe two-way formats such as compression are sufficient in encrypting a password, which is not the case.

Name: Access of Uninitialized Pointer
Author: Rylan
Description: The product accesses or uses a pointer that has not been initialized.
Scenario: A developer may be tasked with the creation and utilization of a pointer. However, utilization of the pointer may be more important, and ensuring it has been properly initialized may be omitted as a result.
Security Impact: read memory, system crash, system restart, execute unauthorized code

Name: Use of Obsolete Function
Author: Rylan
Description: The code uses deprecated or obsolete functions, which suggests that the code has not been actively reviewed or maintained.
Scenario: When reviewing code, developers may encounter outdated methods that worked in the past, but now pose security risks. They may fail to consider cases such as this.

Name: Inappropriate Comment Style
Author: Rylan
Description: The source code uses comment styles or formats that are inconsistent or do not follow expected standards for the product.
Scenario: Developers may utilize comment conventions that have been succesful in the past, but do not meet the requirements of the current project.

Name: Multiple Binds to the Same Port
Author: Rylan
Description: When multiple sockets are allowed to bind to the same port, other services on that port may be stolen or spoofed.
Scenario: When reviewing software uitilizing sockets, developers may confirm functionality, but fail to consider shared sockets across program, potentially leading to a security vulnerability.

Name: Inconsistent Interpretation of HTTP Requests ('HTTP Request/Response Smuggling')
Author: Rylan
Description: The product acts as an intermediary HTTP agent (such as a proxy or firewall) in the data flow between two entities such as a client and server, but it does not interpret malformed HTTP requests or responses in ways that are consistent with how the messages will be processed by those entities that are at the ultimate destination.
Scenario: While designing and intermediary HTTP agent, a developer may design in it a non-functional way for the host failing to realize the specific requirements laid by the host.

Name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Early Validation
Author: Rylan
Description: The product validates input before applying protection mechanisms that modify the input, which could allow an attacker to bypass the validation via dangerous inputs that only arise after the modification.
Scenario: The design of an application may be broken into multiple steps, including validating input and applying protection mechanisms. After implementing, the developer may fail to sequence these components correctly as specified by the main goal.
Security Impact: bypass protection mechanism, execute unauthorized code

Name: Use of Client-Side Authentication
Author: Rylan
Description: A client/server product performs authentication within client code but not in server code, allowing server-side authentication to be bypassed via a modified client that omits the authentication check.
Scenario: Developers may be unaware of the ease client-side authentication provides for malicuous users to spoof authentication.

Name: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
Author: Marvin
Description: Information written to log files can be of a sensitive nature and give valuable guidance to an attacker or expose sensitive user information.
Scenario: Developers may not be aware of the sensitive nature of information that is written to log files, or they may not understand the potential risks of exposing this information to attackers.

Name: Use of Path Manipulation Function without Maximum-sized Buffer
Author: Marvin
Description: The product invokes a function for normalizing paths or file names, but it provides an output buffer that is smaller than the maximum possible size, such as PATH_MAX.
Scenario: Developers may not know that the path is limited in size.
Security Impact: modify memory, execute unauthorized code, system crash, system restart

Name: Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity
Author: Marvin
Description: The product uses a regular expression with an inefficient, possibly exponential worst-case computational complexity that consumes excessive CPU cycles.
Scenario: This may be caused by not fully grasping that regex can exponentially scale in computation and not understanding how regex works in backtracking.

Name: Improper Handling of Parameters
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not properly handle when the expected number of parameters, fields, or arguments is not provided in input, or if those parameters are undefined.
Scenario: This suggests a developer did not check their work and it did not occur to them that an input field could be blank/incorrect/etc.

Name: Invokable Control Element with Signature Containing an Excessive Number of Parameters
Author: Marvin
Description: The product contains a function, subroutine, or method whose signature has an unnecessarily large number of parameters/arguments.
Scenario: The issue is excessive code complexity which can cause issues when humans have a hard time managing/maintaining/tracking everything required for the function.

Name: Firmware Not Updateable
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not provide its users with the ability to update or patch its firmware to address any vulnerabilities or weaknesses that may be present.
Scenario: The company/dev does not envision or provide for ways to protect the product against novel attacks that may occur in the future.
Security Impact: gain privileges, bypass protection mechanism, execute unauthorized code, system crash, system restart

Name: Use of Object without Invoking Destructor Method
Author: Marvin
Description:
The product contains a method that accesses an object but does not later invoke the element's associated finalize/destructor method.
This issue can make the product perform more slowly by retaining memory and/or other resources longer than necessary. If the relevant code is reachable by an attacker, then this performance problem might introduce a vulnerability.
Scenario: The developer may not be aware that objects should be destroyed after they are no longer needed.

Name: Improper Cleanup on Thrown Exception
Author: Marvin
Description:
The product does not clean up its state or incorrectly cleans up its state when an exception is thrown, leading to unexpected state or control flow.
Often, when functions or loops become complicated, some level of resource cleanup is needed throughout execution. Exceptions can disturb the flow of the code and prevent the necessary cleanup from happening.
Scenario: The developer forgot to implement this requirement.

Name: Improper Protection for Outbound Error Messages and Alert Signals
Author: Marvin
Description:
Untrusted agents can disable alerts about signal conditions exceeding limits or the response mechanism that handles such alerts.
Hardware sensors are used to detect whether a device is operating within design limits. The threshold values for these limits are set by hardware fuses or trusted software such as a BIOS. Modification of these limits may be protected by hardware mechanisms.
Scenario: The designers/engineers did not think about securing these warning signals/etc as they are not usually issues, but can be abused for performance/dos.
Security Impact: system crash, system restart, instability, unexpected state

Name: Comparison of Classes by Name
Author: Marvin
Description: The product compares classes by name, which can cause it to use the wrong class when multiple classes can have the same name.
Scenario: Developers may create classes without considering the potential for name collisions and could be unaware of the risks of comparing classes by name if any potential user input is far removed from their logic.

Name: Incorrect Register Defaults or Module Parameters
Author: Marvin
Description: Hardware description language code incorrectly defines register defaults or hardware Intellectual Property (IP) parameters to insecure values.
Scenario: The developer may incorrectly configure values/parameters when creating them.
Security Related Design: resource management, proper initialization, logic design

Name: Use of Externally-Controlled Format String
Author: Marvin
Description: The product uses a function that accepts a format string as an argument, but the format string originates from an external source.
Scenario: The core issue in this vulnerability stems from a developer's potential lack of understanding about the dangers of using format strings (like in C's printf family of functions) constructed from untrusted external input. Without this knowledge, they might inadvertently create code that leaves the application open to malicious manipulation.

Name: Incorrect Ownership Assignment
Author: Marvin
Description:
The product assigns an owner to a resource, but the owner is outside of the intended control sphere.
This may allow the resource to be manipulated by actors outside of the intended control sphere.
Scenario: This is an error made in management of user roles.
Security Impact: read application data, modify application data

Name: Comparing instead of Assigning
Author: Marvin
Description:
The code uses an operator for comparison when the intention was to perform an assignment.
In many languages, the compare statement is very close in appearance to the assignment statement; they are often confused.
Scenario: The developer may sleepily mistake the operator necessary for the problem at hand.

Name: Use of Inherently Dangerous Function
Author: Marvin
Description: The product calls a function that can never be guaranteed to work safely.
Scenario: The developer does not know that the function is not safe.
Security Related Design: automated static analysis, api errors, function errors

Name: SQL Injection: Hibernate
Author: Marvin
Description: Using Hibernate to execute a dynamic SQL statement built with user-controlled input can allow an attacker to modify the statement's meaning or to execute arbitrary SQL commands.
Scenario: The developer is not aware that any user input should be sanitized before it can be used with an sql statement.

Name: Use of Weak Credentials
Author: Marvin
Description: The product uses weak credentials (such as a default key or hard-coded password) that can be calculated, derived, reused, or guessed by an attacker.
Scenario: There was a failure in the planning/execution in which a decision was made to use weak credentials that can be easily cracked.

Name: Improper Update of Reference Count
Author: Marvin
Description: The product uses a reference count to manage a resource, but it does not update or incorrectly updates the reference count.
Scenario: Developers may make incorrect assumptions about object lifetimes, how reference counts should be managed in different scenarios, or the interactions between code components.
Security Impact: resource consumption, system crash, system restart

Name: Use of Incorrect Byte Ordering
Author: Marvin
Description: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not account for byte ordering (e.g. big-endian and little-endian) when processing the input, causing an incorrect number or value to be used.
Scenario: A developer might not understand how byte ordering works across different systems.

Name: Use of Unmaintained Third Party Components
Author: Marvin
Description: The product relies on third-party components that are not actively supported or maintained by the original developer or a trusted proxy for the original developer.
Scenario: This is an issue with choosing to use poor third party libraries and not choosing to prioritize replacing them when they are known to be outdated/poorly maintained.
Security Related Design: expired code, unmaintained code, automated static analysis

Name: Improper Handling of Mixed Encoding
Author: Marvin
Description: The product does not properly handle when the same input uses several different (mixed) encodings.
Scenario: It was not caught in review that the functionality takes input that could have weird encoding stuff. The CWE recommends to always assume input is malicious and to use and specify a common encoding.
